retrieved_evidence.text: WASHINGTON — Roger Stone drew fresh attention after Axios reported he urged President Trump to keep Tulsi Gabbard in her role. Stone later confirmed on X that he intervened. (April 10, 2026)

Axios reported in recent days that longtime Republican operative Roger Stone privately urged then President Donald Trump to keep Tulsi Gabbard in her existing role at the time, according to people said to be familiar with the matter. The outreach was described as part of informal conversations around personnel decisions rather than a formal process. Individuals cited in the report did not specify where or when the exchanges took place, and officials involved have not publicly detailed how much weight was given to the recommendation, which remains one of several accounts circulating about internal discussions.

Roger Stone, a longtime political operative and ally of Donald Trump, has remained a visible figure at Republican-aligned events, media interviews and activist gatherings in recent years, often speaking from podiums bearing party or campaign branding. In recent reports he has described using his access to Trump to advocate on personnel matters, including saying that he personally urged the president not to move against Tulsi Gabbard when her standing inside the administration was reportedly under pressure, a claim that reflects the informal channels through which advisers sometimes sought to influence White House decisions.

Roger Stone acknowledged on the social media platform X this week that he had personally intervened in discussions related to Tulsi Gabbard’s position, according to his own public posts. In the brief message, Stone stated that he had communicated his views to then President Donald Trump, without elaborating on the precise circumstances or format of the exchange. Observers noted that his comments appeared to confirm earlier media accounts while leaving unanswered questions about the extent of his involvement, and no additional documentation was immediately provided.

Recent reporting on Roger Stone’s communications with then President Donald Trump regarding Tulsi Gabbard has drawn renewed attention to Stone’s activities, according to media analysts. The combination of an Axios account and Stone’s own confirmation on social media has prompted fresh discussion among commentators about his access to senior figures and his continuing role in conservative politics. Observers say the development has shifted the focus back onto Stone’s behind-the-scenes efforts, while officials have declined to comment on any internal deliberations that may have followed.

People familiar with the discussions have characterized Roger Stone’s involvement in the matter as influence and advocacy rather than any formal role in government decision-making, according to accounts shared in recent days. These sources said Stone’s input came through informal channels and reflected his longstanding status as a political adviser and outside ally, rather than participation in official staffing procedures. Officials have not indicated that he held any decision-making authority in the process, and several aspects of how his views were considered remain unclear.

Background references to Roger Stone in coverage of the latest disclosures have largely been limited to broad descriptions, such as labeling him a longtime ally of Donald Trump, a Republican political operative and an adviser in past campaigns, according to media monitoring groups. Editors involved in recent stories say they have chosen to highlight only these high-level characterizations to provide context while avoiding more extensive biographical detail. The aim, they note, is to situate Stone’s comments within his established public profile without expanding into unrelated history.

In coverage of the episode involving Roger Stone and the Trump administration, Tulsi Gabbard is being described in neutral terms corresponding to her role at the time, according to editors and reporters familiar with the reporting standards. They note that references to her position avoid adding detailed job titles or responsibilities that have not been separately confirmed, reflecting an effort to keep biographical descriptions strictly aligned with publicly verifiable information. The approach is intended to focus attention on the documented interactions rather than on speculative characterizations of her career.

PHOENIX — The AP photo desk published images from Turning Point Action’s “Build the Red Wall” rally as Trump campaigned for the 2026 midterms. Roger Stone has recently appeared at pro-Trump events and on talk shows.

Tulsi Gabbard, a former Democratic member of the U.S. House of Representatives from Hawaii and one-time presidential candidate, has frequently appeared at official functions and public forums in suits or other formal attire, underscoring her profile as a national political figure. In recent accounts referenced by commentators, she is identified as the official whose position Roger Stone says he sought to protect by appealing directly to President Donald Trump, illustrating how disputes over her role within the administration intersected with broader debates about bipartisan outreach and unconventional appointments.

So, “believe all women” without proof? 

Believe a woman who was raped once, and didn’t go to the police. 

Believe a woman who years later went out with the same married guy AGAIN — who raped her, AGAIN — and didn’t go to the police. AGAIN. 

You know who knew? 

Roger stone. 
Katie Porter

You know who didn’t know? 

Her boyfriend didn’t know. 

So, yeah, proof IS necessary.
